title: Renewing Arizona Families: Why Strong Families are Central to Arizona’s Future | Institute for Family Studies
gist: A state-specific IFS report (Wilcox, Bailey, Anderson, Gentala, Trent) documenting how intact married families predict better child education, mental health, and lower poverty across Arizona, that marriage drives adult homeownership and lower welfare use, that Arizona's family-structure metrics and fertility lag the U.S., and proposing a detailed policy agenda: Success Sequence education, marriage-penalty fixes, premarital-education incentives, boy-friendly schooling, child-safety tech laws, and faith-community partnership.

insights.claim: 85% of Arizona children living with intact married parents earn A's and B's versus 64% from single-mother homes; net of controls, children from non-intact families are about 91% more likely to earn poor grades.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Establishes family structure as a primary driver of academic outcomes, the report's foundational evidentiary claim.
insights.quote: 85% of children living with their intact married parents received A’s and B’s compared to 64% of those from single-mother homes

insights.claim: In a multivariate model, Arizona districts with the highest share of married-parent families have a 43-percentage-point reading-proficiency advantage over the lowest-share districts — larger than the 27-point advantage of the highest-earning districts over the lowest-earning.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: The headline finding that family structure out-predicts income for educational outcomes — central to the report's policy argument.
insights.quote: the married-parent household advantage is larger than income

insights.claim: Higley Unified (Gilbert) has 77% married-parent households and 60% ELA proficiency, while Flowing Wells has 54% married parents and 35% proficiency despite spending ~60% more per student on classroom support ($1,138 vs $734).
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: A concrete district comparison suggesting per-pupil spending is dominated by family structure — a politically charged education-finance implication.
insights.quote: despite the district spending about 60% more per-student on classroom support than Higley
insights.locator: For instance, 60% of students in Higley

insights.claim: Net of controls, Arizona students from non-intact families are 33% more likely to have parents contacted by the school for behavioral or learning issues.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Extends the family-structure effect to behavioral/discipline outcomes.
insights.quote: 33% more likely to have their parents contacted by their school

insights.claim: Net of controls, Arizona girls are 60% more likely and boys 57% more likely to be diagnosed with depression when raised in a home without both parents.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Ties family instability to the youth mental-health crisis, broadening the case beyond economics.
insights.quote: girls are 60% more likely to be depressed and boys are 57% more likely to be depressed

insights.claim: Net of controls, Arizona school-aged children in non-intact families are 274% more likely to experience one or more adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) than peers in intact families; 43% of Arizona children have experienced one or more ACEs vs 39.8% nationally.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: The largest effect size in the report, framing intact marriage as a powerful protective factor against childhood trauma.
insights.quote: 274% more likely to experience at least one or more of the following ACEs

insights.claim: Just 10% of Arizona children in married intact families fall below the official poverty line, versus 38% with single mothers and 41% in cohabiting families — indicating the parent-child relationship, not merely number of adults, is protective.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Quantifies marriage as the dominant anti-poverty factor for children, central to the policy case.
insights.quote: just 10% of Arizona children living in married, intact families fall below the official poverty line

insights.claim: Family structure has become a stronger predictor of Arizona child poverty over time: children from unmarried/non-intact homes were 54% more likely to be poor than peers with married parents in the late 2000s, rising to 136% for 2020-2025.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: A trend claim — the marriage-poverty link is strengthening, raising the stakes of family decline.
insights.quote: that number has risen to 136% for 2020 to 2025
insights.locator: We also conducted a multivariate analysis with an interaction term

insights.claim: University of Arizona College of Medicine-Phoenix research found divorced, widowed, or never-married people had a 42% greater risk of cardiovascular disease and 16% greater risk of coronary artery disease than married people.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Adds a health-economics dimension to the marriage premium, relevant to healthcare-cost arguments.
insights.quote: a 42 percent greater risk of developing cardiovascular disease

insights.claim: Marriage is by far the strongest predictor of homeownership among Arizona adults 35+, surpassing income, education, and race; 51% of married adults in the bottom income quartile own homes versus 36% of unmarried adults in the top income quartile.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Marriage as a homeownership driver links family policy to housing-market demand, a cross-sector signal.
insights.quote: being married is by far the strongest predictor of homeownership in Arizona
insights.locator: Figure 18. Change in odds of homeownership

insights.claim: Arizona middle-aged homeownership fell from 83% (1980) to a 53% bottom in 2017, recovering to 62% today, with the decline concentrated among unmarried adults — widening the married/unmarried homeownership gap.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Marriage decline as a structural driver of the homeownership gap, relevant to long-run housing demand composition.
insights.quote: homeownership among middle-aged adults bottomed out in 2017 with 53%

insights.claim: Marriage strongly predicts lower welfare use: only 15% of prime-aged married Arizonans receive public benefits versus 24% of unmarried men and 34% of unmarried women (food stamps, Medicaid, cash welfare, or SSDI).
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Fiscal angle — marriage reduces welfare dependency, a core argument for state pro-marriage spending.
insights.quote: only 15% of prime-aged (25-55) married men and women in Arizona receive welfare compared to 24% of unmarried men and 34% of unmarried women

insights.claim: At the county level, violent crime and poverty are generally lower where more prime-aged adults are married: Maricopa (53% married) has a 9% poverty rate while Apache (35% married) has 29%.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Community-level association linking marriage rates to crime and poverty, citing Chetty/Price mobility research.
insights.quote: in Maricopa county, 53% of men and women ages 25-54 are married, and the poverty rate is just 9 percent
insights.locator: Arizona Families and the Common Good

insights.claim: Arizona ranks 35th of 50 states on the IFS Family Structure Index (marriage, fertility, intact families), having risen from 39th since 2020; 62% of Arizona children live with married parents versus 63% nationally.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Benchmarks Arizona's family health against peers, framing the improvement-but-still-lagging narrative.
insights.quote: Arizona ranks 35th in the Union

insights.claim: Arizona's marriage rate fell from 45 newlyweds per 1,000 single adults in 2000 to ~30 by 2013, average first-marriage age rose from 28.8 (2008) to 30.2 (2024), and the unmarried birth rate is 45.6% vs 40% nationally.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Documents the marriage retreat driving the report's urgency.
insights.quote: the marriage rate fell to about 30 in 2013

insights.claim: Arizona's total fertility rate has fallen from well above the national average to ~1.60 (national level), far below replacement; the authors warn the state cannot rely on immigration since Mexico and Latin America also have sub-replacement fertility.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Sub-replacement fertility plus drying immigration is a long-run labor-supply and fiscal warning for the state.
insights.quote: the total fertility rate has plummeted and is about equal to the national level at 1.60

insights.claim: Hispanic children with at least one foreign-born parent are more likely to live in a married family than those with only U.S.-born parents, suggesting U.S. acculturation discourages marriage among Hispanic adults.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: A counterintuitive acculturation finding with demographic implications as Arizona becomes more Hispanic.
insights.quote: Something about being born and raised in the United States for Hispanic adults may be discouraging marriage
insights.locator: Nativity and ethnicity are also related to family structure

insights.claim: 97% of young adults who follow the Success Sequence (high school, full-time job, marriage before children) avoid poverty by their late 20s-30s, and 50% lower odds of emotional distress — the report urges Arizona schools to teach it.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: The flagship policy recommendation; ties this report to the broader IFS Success Sequence curriculum push.
insights.quote: a stunning 97% of young men and women who follow this simple sequence succeed in avoiding poverty

insights.claim: The report proposes a $20 million Arizona public-education/media campaign to raise the marriage rate, funded partly by allocating 10% of the state's TANF budget to marriage and two-parent-family activities.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: A concrete, sizable state-spending proposal indicating the scale of intervention being lobbied for.
insights.quote: Create a $20 million public education and media campaign
insights.locator: Strengthening Marriage in the Grand Canyon State

insights.claim: The report recommends eliminating Arizona's Head of Household filing status (which creates a marriage penalty for single parents) paired with raising the Dependent Tax Credit from $100 to $305 for under-17 children, plus $1,000 TANF grants to married families with under-5 children facing a documented marriage penalty.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Specific tax-code reforms — the actionable fiscal mechanics of pro-marriage policy.
insights.quote: pair HoH elimination with an increase in the Dependent Tax Credit from $100 to $305

insights.claim: The report cites Texas's 2007 premarital-education program as correlating with a 1.5% statewide divorce-rate decrease and Utah's 2008 awareness campaign that raised premarital-education program awareness from 17% to 38%, recommending Arizona waive marriage-license fees for couples completing approved premarital courses.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Evidence-backed precedent for premarital-education incentives, a low-cost policy lever.
insights.quote: correlated with a 1.5% decrease in the divorce rate statewide

themes.theme: Boys, men, and the gender gap

insights.claim: Arizona's gender gap is stark: 67% of the lowest GPAs among schoolchildren are boys and 60% of full-time college students are female; the report recommends boy-friendly schooling, doubling Career and Technical Education funding, and a 20% sin tax on gaming.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Frames declining male prospects as a marriageability and family-formation problem, proposing a notable gaming sin-tax.
insights.quote: 67% of the lowest GPAs for school children in the state are among boys

insights.claim: The report recommends Arizona require age verification for human-like AI, pass an App Store Accountability Act requiring age verification and parental consent for app downloads, limit classroom screen time (following Utah), and ban under-16s from social media (following Australia).
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: A concrete child-online-safety regulatory agenda with implications for app-store operators and social platforms.
insights.quote: Require age verification for users of human-like AI and restrict access to adults.

insights.claim: The report cites the Character.AI/Google lawsuit settlement over Sewell Setzer III (a 14-year-old who killed himself after an AI companion's manipulation) and states AI 'alignment' is currently unsolved and may never be solved.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Cites a precedent-setting AI-liability settlement and a strong claim about alignment risk — relevant to AI-companion regulatory exposure.
insights.quote: “alignment,” the technical challenge of ensuring that AI systems pursue pro-social ends, being currently unsolved
insights.locator: Require age verification for users of human-like AI

insights.claim: Weekly church attenders have divorce rates 25-50% lower than non-attenders, women who attend weekly are 60-75% less likely to have a nonmarital birth, and Arizona counties with higher congregation membership show 6-9 percentage points lower child poverty after controls.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Positions religious participation as social capital that partly compensates for economic disadvantage and supports family stability.
insights.quote: Weekly church attenders have divorce rates 25-50% lower than those who rarely or never attend

insights.claim: Arizona religious participation is declining generationally — only 22% of adults under 30 attend weekly versus 35% of those over 50 — while the state's ~4,000-5,000 congregations represent a largely untapped family-support infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, Grant Bailey, Sophie Anderson, Peter Gentala, Bob Trent
insights.so_what: Generational faith decline threatens the family-stability buffer; congregations are framed as scalable, low-cost family-support assets.
insights.quote: only 22% of Arizona adults under 30 attend religious services weekly, compared to 35% of those over 50
